summary: This advice column addresses four reader questions spanning career transitions, social etiquette, statistical modeling of dating, and the pursuit of hedonism. Cathy O'Neil offers characteristically blunt guidance on each topic, from warning about academia's bias against career gaps to debunking a reader's belief that wealth automatically attracts romantic partners. She applies quantitative thinking even to dating advice, discussing pheromones and conditional probabilities of 'getting laid.' The column showcases her ability to combine mathematical frameworks with sharp social commentary and no-nonsense personal advice.
key_points: Academia, particularly in quantitative fields like math and economics, unfairly penalizes career gaps, disproportionately affecting women
Self-perception and confidence create feedback loops in dating - feeling attractive makes you act attractive, which increases your chances
Wealth and accomplishments don't automatically translate to meaningful relationships; treating people as commodities signals existing superficiality
Real hedonistic enjoyment requires genuine human connections, not transactional relationships with strangers
Social support networks (wingmen/wingwomen) can help break negative cycles in dating outcomes
money_quotes: If math, as a community, were serious about attracting good women, they'd change this bias. But I don't see that happening soon.
This is a great example of a question that says more about the questioner than anything about the question.
If you feel unattractive, you don't act like a sexy thang and people are less likely to approach you.
If you've gotten laid recently, you feel sexy, which makes you act like a sexy person, which is hot in itself.
Please let me be the first person to tell you that you're already quite superficial. Congratulations!
Just the way you're talking about 'girls' makes me kind of gag, as if they're lego parts that can be bought, traded, and sold.
The sneaky little thing about really enjoying a hedonistic lifestyle is, in my opinion, that you have real connections with the other people in your company.
It's not fun to do stupid sexy things with strangers whose motivations you don't know, especially if you're young and rich, because even if you don't know, I will.
key_insight: Authentic human connection cannot be bought or gamed with status; the shortcuts people seek in relationships often reveal the superficiality they claim to lack.
